subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Operant Conditioning and Stimulus Discrimination (AAQ)
stimulus:
Introduction
Dogs often comfort their owners by making visual and/or physical contact when the owners cry or by providing help to their owners when they are sick. The study examined whether a dog's reactions to a person's emotions differ based on the dog's prior experience with the person (owner versus stranger).
Participants
Researchers recruited community members with nonaggressive dogs via email. Of those recruited, 16 dog and owner pairs participated in this study. The owners ranged in age from 25 to 60 years (mean age = 47.06 years; standard deviation = 11.89 years), and the dogs ranged in age from 2 years to 13 years (mean age = 6.87 years; standard deviation = 3.35 years). The length of time the owners had their dogs before the study began ranged from 2 months to 13 years (mean = 5.70 years; standard deviation = 3.69 years). The study included the following dog breeds, with the number of each in parentheses: Lab mix (3), black Lab (1), terrier mix (2), corgi or corgi mix (3), French bulldog (1), Jack Russell terrier (1), miniature pinscher (1), miniature schnauzer (1), Shih Tzu (2), and wirehaired pointing griffon (1).
Method
Each dog owner received and signed a consent form. Researchers tested each dog at their owner's house, and the dogs received dog biscuits as compensation for participation in the study.
All dog and owner pairs participated in all four trials of the study. This is called a "within-subjects" design, which means that researchers observe each participant in every condition of the study so that a participant can be directly compared to themselves across conditions. The "within-subjects" design serves the same purpose as random assignment.
A researcher who was unfamiliar with the dogs before the study played the role of the stranger. Upon entering each participant's home, this "stranger-researcher" ignored the dogs by not interacting with them in any way. The stranger-researcher asked the owner to follow the same set of instructions during each trial:
"When you are asked to cry, please pretend to cry to the best of your ability for 20 seconds. The researcher will tell you when to begin and when to stop. When you are asked to laugh, please pretend to laugh to the best of your ability for 20 seconds. When you are acting, please use approximately the same volume. Also, please do not refer to your dog by name, look directly at them, or initiate or reciprocate physical contact during the study."
The owners and the stranger-researcher stayed seated and moved their bodies naturally while they acted out the different emotional states, but they stayed seated. Each dog participated in four separate 20-second-long trials in which (1) the owner cried, (2) the stranger-researcher cried, (3) the owner laughed, and (4) the stranger-researcher laughed.
The order of these trials was counterbalanced across dogs, meaning that each dog completed the four trials in a randomized order. Before the trials began (baseline), in between each trial, and at the end of the fourth trial, the stranger-researcher and the owner had a light-hearted conversation for 2 minutes to reset the emotional experience for the dogs and the owners.
Results and Discussion
Researchers focused on two different kinds of dog behaviors. Person-oriented dog behaviors included looking at a person (either the owner or the stranger-researcher), making contact with a person (touching the person in some way), approaching a person, and vocalizing at a person (barking, whining, etc.). Non-person-oriented dog behaviors included passive behavior (e.g., lying down or sitting), walking, solitary play, and general vocalizing (not directed at a person).
The total number of person-oriented behaviors from the dogs for each trial behavior the owners and researcher-strangers acted out is presented in the table.
Total Number of Person-Oriented Dog Behaviors for Each Trial
| Behavior | Dogs Looking | Dogs Making Contact | Dogs Approaching | Dogs Vocalizing | Total Person-Oriented Behaviors |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Baseline | 5 | 3 | 2 | 2 | 12 |
| Owner crying | 20 | 15 | 6 | 1 | 42 |
| Owner laughing | 11 | 2 | 2 | 0 | 15 |
| Stranger crying | 24 | 11 | 8 | 3 | 46 |
| Stranger laughing | 11 | 7 | 2 | 0 | 20 |
| Totals | 71 | 38 | 20 | 6 | 135 |
The graph displays the means for the number of person-oriented behaviors for the three types of emotions. The results are statistically significant (p < 0.001).1
Mean of Person-Oriented Behaviors for Emotion Trials
The results show that the crying trial elicited significantly more person-oriented behaviors than the laughing and talking trials. The mean of person-oriented behaviors when the owner and stranger-researcher were crying significantly differed from the means for laughing and talking, but the means for laughing and talking did not significantly differ from each other. Importantly, dogs primarily engaged with the individual who was crying, regardless of whether they were the owner or the stranger-researcher. In the owner-crying trial, dogs demonstrated person-oriented behaviors 75% of the time, and in the stranger-researcher-crying trial, dogs demonstrated the person-oriented behaviors 73% of the time.
Meyers-Manor, J. E., & Botten, M. L. (2020). A shoulder to cry on: Heart rate variability and empathetic behavioral responses to crying and laughing in dogs. Canadian Journal of Experimental Psychology / Revue canadienne de psychologie expérimentale, 74(3), 235–243.
1: p values indicate statistical significance. A p value of less than 0.05 is generally considered significant and increases the likelihood that the difference in means is due to the procedures of the study.
The question has three parts: Part A, Part B, and Part C. Use the three sources provided to answer each part of the question.
For Part B and Part C, you must cite the source that you used to answer the question. You can do this in two different ways:
• Parenthetical Citation: For example: "...(Source 1)."
• Embedded Citation: For example: "According to Source 1..."
Write the response to each part of the question in complete sentences. Use appropriate psychological terminology.

parts.prompt: Identify the research method used in the study.

scoring_points.description: Accurately identifies the research method used in the study

required_elements: "The researchers used an experiment."
alternatives.correct_example: "The researchers used an experiment."

required_elements: "The researchers used a within-subject experiment."
alternatives.correct_example: "The researchers used a within-subject experiment."

required_elements: "The researchers studied whether a dog's reaction to a person's emotions differ based on the dog's experience with the person."
alternatives.correct_example: "The researchers studied whether a dog's reaction to a person's emotions differ based on the dog's experience with the person."

scoring_points.official_rubric: Accurately identifies the research method as an experiment. Identifying a case study, naturalistic observation, or only describing the study topic without naming the method does not earn this point. A response that names the experiment but directly contradicts it (e.g., 'experimental method but specifically a naturalistic observation') also does not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: The researchers used an experiment.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly identifies the research method as an experiment.

sample_responses.response: The researchers used a within-subject experiment.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly identifies the specific type of experiment (within-subjects design) used in the study.

sample_responses.response: The research method used in the study is a case study since the researchers are examining a dog's reactions to a person's emotions.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; case study is an incorrect identification of the research method.

sample_responses.response: The researchers studied whether a dog's reaction to a person's emotions differ based on the dog's experience with the person.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; describes what was studied rather than identifying the research method.

sample_responses.response: The research method in this study is the experimental method, but they were specifically using a naturalistic observation.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; directly contradicts itself — calling it experimental and then naturalistic observation in the same response disqualifies the point.

parts.prompt: State the operational definition of person-oriented dog behaviors.

scoring_points.description: States a measurable or quantifiable definition of the identified variable as used in the study

required_elements: "The researchers studied how emotionally close the dogs were to their owners."
alternatives.correct_example: "The researchers studied how emotionally close the dogs were to their owners."

required_elements: "The operational definition of person-oriented dog behavior is the 2-minute lighthearted conversation the stranger- researcher and the owner had."
alternatives.correct_example: "The operational definition of person-oriented dog behavior is the 2-minute lighthearted conversation the stranger- researcher and the owner had."

required_elements: "The operational definition of person-oriented dog behaviors includes passive behavior like lying down or sitting."
alternatives.correct_example: "The operational definition of person-oriented dog behaviors includes passive behavior like lying down or sitting."

required_elements: "The researchers operationally defined person-oriented dog behaviors as the number of times they looked at the person or barked at the person."
alternatives.correct_example: "The researchers operationally defined person-oriented dog behaviors as the number of times they looked at the person or barked at the person."

required_elements: "The researchers defined person-oriented dog behaviors as making contact or approaching the people."
alternatives.correct_example: "The researchers defined person-oriented dog behaviors as making contact or approaching the people."

scoring_points.official_rubric: States that person-oriented dog behaviors are defined as the number of times a dog looked at a person, contacted a person, approached a person, and/or vocalized at a person in the study. Vague emotional descriptions, references to the baseline conversation period, or listing non-person-oriented behaviors (e.g., lying down) do not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: The researchers operationally defined person-oriented dog behaviors as the number of times they looked at the person or barked at the person.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; identifies measurable, specific behaviors (looking, barking/vocalizing) that the researchers counted as person-oriented.

sample_responses.response: The researchers defined person-oriented dog behaviors as making contact or approaching the people.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; identifies two measurable components of the operational definition (contact, approaching).

sample_responses.response: The researchers studied how emotionally close the dogs were to their owners.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; describes an emotional concept, not a measurable or quantifiable operational definition.

sample_responses.response: The operational definition of person-oriented dog behavior is the 2-minute lighthearted conversation the stranger-researcher and the owner had.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; the 2-minute conversation was a between-trial reset procedure, not the operational definition of person-oriented behaviors.

sample_responses.response: The operational definition of person-oriented dog behaviors includes passive behavior like lying down or sitting.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; lying down and sitting are explicitly categorized in the study as non-person-oriented behaviors.

parts.prompt: Describe what the mean of the person-oriented behaviors indicates for the laughing trials as compared to the talking trials.

scoring_points.description: Accurately describes what the identified statistic indicates in relation to the study

required_elements: "The mean of the laughing group is 1.3 and the mean for
alternatives.correct_example: "The mean of the laughing group is 1.3 and the mean for

required_elements: "The dogs showed more person-oriented behaviors to people the talking group is .75."
alternatives.correct_example: "The dogs showed more person-oriented behaviors to people the talking group is .75."

required_elements: "The mean of the laughing group is significantly more than the mean of the talking group." [No explanation of what that indicates in the study.]
alternatives.correct_example: "The mean of the laughing group is significantly more than the mean of the talking group." [No explanation of what that indicates in the study.]

required_elements: "The mean is the average of the responses in the study. The dogs in the laughing group had a mean of 1.4 person- oriented behaviors." who were laughing than those who were just talking."
alternatives.correct_example: "The mean is the average of the responses in the study. The dogs in the laughing group had a mean of 1.4 person- oriented behaviors." who were laughing than those who were just talking."

required_elements: "The dogs responded more to laughing than to talking."
alternatives.correct_example: "The dogs responded more to laughing than to talking."

required_elements: "Since the difference between the means of the laughing and talking trials was not significant, this means that the difference in means was probably due to chance."
alternatives.correct_example: "Since the difference between the means of the laughing and talking trials was not significant, this means that the difference in means was probably due to chance."

required_elements: "There was not a significant difference between the two. This just indicates that the animal's response was not due to laughing or talking."
alternatives.correct_example: "There was not a significant difference between the two. This just indicates that the animal's response was not due to laughing or talking."

scoring_points.official_rubric: Accurately describes what the mean of person-oriented behaviors indicates for the laughing trials compared to talking trials. Either interpretation earns the point: (1) that dogs showed more person-oriented behaviors to laughing than talking, or (2) that the means were not significantly different, indicating the difference was likely due to chance. Simply restating the mean values without relating them to the study does not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: The dogs showed more person-oriented behaviors to people who were laughing than those who were just talking.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; accurately describes what the larger laughing mean indicates in relation to the study.

sample_responses.response: The dogs responded more to laughing than to talking.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; concisely and accurately describes what the difference in means indicates.

sample_responses.response: Since the difference between the means of the laughing and talking trials was not significant, this means that the difference in means was probably due to chance.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; accurately interprets the non-significant difference between means in context of the study.

sample_responses.response: There was not a significant difference between the two. This just indicates that the animal's response was not due to laughing or talking.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; accurately explains that the lack of significant difference indicates neither laughing nor talking specifically drove the behavior.

sample_responses.response: The mean of the laughing group is 1.3 and the mean for the talking group is .75.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; merely restates the mean values without explaining what they indicate in relation to the study.

sample_responses.response: The mean of the laughing group is significantly more than the mean of the talking group.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; states a comparison but provides no explanation of what that difference indicates in the study.

parts.prompt: Identify at least one ethical guideline applied by the researchers.

scoring_points.description: Accurately identifies at least one ethical guideline applied by researchers in the study

required_elements: "It's important to researchers to act ethically when
alternatives.correct_example: "It's important to researchers to act ethically when

required_elements: "The researchers obtained informed consent from the dog conducting a study." owners."
alternatives.correct_example: "The researchers obtained informed consent from the dog conducting a study." owners."

required_elements: "The researchers should have obtained the dog's consent."
alternatives.correct_example: "The researchers should have obtained the dog's consent."

required_elements: "The researchers protected the confidentiality of the
alternatives.correct_example: "The researchers protected the confidentiality of the

required_elements: "The researchers made sure they did no harm to the animals, who could not give consent to be studied." participants in the study."
alternatives.correct_example: "The researchers made sure they did no harm to the animals, who could not give consent to be studied." participants in the study."

required_elements: "The researchers gave dog biscuits as compensation, which
alternatives.correct_example: "The researchers gave dog biscuits as compensation, which

required_elements: "The researchers had the stranger and the dog owner have a cheerful conversation between trials to reset the emotional tone of the experience for both the dogs and the owners. This demonstrates that the researchers took steps to protect the participants from harm."
alternatives.correct_example: "The researchers had the stranger and the dog owner have a cheerful conversation between trials to reset the emotional tone of the experience for both the dogs and the owners. This demonstrates that the researchers took steps to protect the participants from harm."

scoring_points.official_rubric: Accurately identifies informed consent or doing no unnecessary harm to the animals as ethical guidelines applied by researchers in the study. Responses that identify guidelines not mentioned in the study (e.g., debriefing), guidelines not applied, or non-ethical features of the study (e.g., giving biscuits as compensation) do not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: The researchers obtained informed consent from the dog owners.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; the study states owners received and signed a consent form, confirming informed consent was obtained.

sample_responses.response: The researchers made sure they did no harm to the animals, who could not give consent to be studied.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly identifies the ethical guideline of doing no unnecessary harm, applied here to the animal participants.

sample_responses.response: The researchers had the stranger and the dog owner have a cheerful conversation between trials to reset the emotional tone of the experience for both the dogs and the owners. This demonstrates that the researchers took steps to protect the participants from harm.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly identifies the between-trial reset procedure as evidence of the researchers' effort to minimize harm to participants.

sample_responses.response: The researchers should have obtained the dog's consent.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; dogs cannot provide consent — this is not an ethical guideline that was applied in the study.

sample_responses.response: The researchers gave dog biscuits as compensation, which is unethical.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; providing dog biscuits as compensation is not unethical and is not an ethical guideline applied in the study.

parts.prompt: Explain the extent to which the research findings may or may not be generalizable using specific and relevant evidence from the study.
parts.scoring_notes: • If students assert the study is "partly" generalizable or asserts the study is both generalizable and not generalizable, their evidence must support their assertion by showing ways the study is generalizable and is not generalizable to earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Explains the extent to which the study is generalizable using specific and relevant evidence referencing participant variables from the study

required_elements: "The study yielded some good results."
alternatives.correct_example: "The study yielded some good results."

required_elements: "The study is generalizable."
alternatives.correct_example: "The study is generalizable."

required_elements: "The study is generalizable because they had a large sample size."
alternatives.correct_example: "The study is generalizable because they had a large sample size."

required_elements: "The study is generalizable to dogs of all kinds because they used several different kinds of dogs in the study."
alternatives.correct_example: "The study is generalizable to dogs of all kinds because they used several different kinds of dogs in the study."

required_elements: "The study is only generalizable to nonaggressive dog breeds because although they used a variety of dogs, they recruited only nonaggressive dogs for their sample."
alternatives.correct_example: "The study is only generalizable to nonaggressive dog breeds because although they used a variety of dogs, they recruited only nonaggressive dogs for their sample."

required_elements: "The study is not generalizable to dog owners younger than 25 or older than 60."
alternatives.correct_example: "The study is not generalizable to dog owners younger than 25 or older than 60."

scoring_points.official_rubric: Must both (1) propose a claim about generalizability referencing a population and (2) use specific evidence from participant information in the study. Sample size alone is not a participant variable. Responses that make a claim without evidence or cite evidence without making a generalizability claim do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: The study is generalizable to dogs of all kinds because they used several different kinds of dogs in the study.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; makes a claim about generalizability and supports it with specific participant evidence (variety of dog breeds used).

sample_responses.response: The study is only generalizable to nonaggressive dog breeds because although they used a variety of dogs, they recruited only nonaggressive dogs for their sample.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly limits generalizability based on the specific participant characteristic (nonaggressive dogs only).

sample_responses.response: The study is not generalizable to dog owners younger than 25 or older than 60.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly limits generalizability based on the specific age range of owner participants in the study.

sample_responses.response: The study yielded some good results.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; makes no claim about generalizability and references no participant evidence.

sample_responses.response: The study is generalizable because they had a large sample size.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; sample size is not a participant variable that impacts generalizability.

parts.prompt: Explain how at least one of the research findings supports or refutes the idea that dogs' expressions of the person-oriented behaviors demonstrate stimulus discrimination in operant conditioning.

scoring_points.description: Partial credit (1 pt): Uses evidence from the study but does not fully explain how the concept is supported or refuted, or explains without using a specific research finding.

required_elements: Uses the results of the study but does not explain how the psychological concept or hypothesis is supported or refuted

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: Uses the results of the study but does not explain how stimulus discrimination in operant conditioning is supported or refuted. OR explains that stimulus discrimination is supported or refuted but uses no results from the study. Inaccurate interpretation of results also limits a response to 1 point.

sample_responses.response: The study showed that dogs can tell the difference between the emotions their owners show.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns 1 point (explanation without evidence); explains the concept but uses no specific result from the study.

sample_responses.response: The study shows that the dogs' responses to crying were significantly more than when the people were laughing or talking.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns 1 point (evidence without explanation); cites a result but does not explain how it supports or refutes stimulus discrimination.

sample_responses.response: The study shows the dogs responded to crying more than laughing or talking only to their owners, showing that they discriminate which type of emotion to respond to.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns 1 point; uses evidence but inaccurately interprets results — the dogs actually responded similarly to the owner and the stranger crying (75% vs. 73%), not just to the owner.

sample_responses.response: The study shows that stimulus discrimination happens in the study.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn any points; does not explain how any research finding supports or refutes the concept.

scoring_points.description: Full credit (2 pts): Uses a specific result from the study to accurately explain how the results support or refute the psychological concept, with correctly interpreted evidence.

required_elements: Uses the results of the study but does not explain how the psychological concept or hypothesis is supported or refuted

common_traps: Must accurately interpret results — not just cite evidence
Must explicitly identify the psychological concept/theory from the AP Psychology CED
scoring_points.official_rubric: 2 points: Uses at least one specific result from the study to accurately explain how the results support or refute stimulus discrimination in operant conditioning. Results must be accurately interpreted.

sample_responses.response: The study shows that the dogs showed person-oriented behaviors more toward people who were crying than people who were laughing or talking, which supports the idea that dogs have learned to discriminate.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns 2 points; uses a specific result (more behaviors toward crying than laughing/talking) and accurately explains how it supports stimulus discrimination.

sample_responses.response: The dogs responded more to crying than laughing, so they discriminated between the emotions.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns 2 points; concisely uses a specific result to explain how the dogs demonstrated stimulus discrimination between emotional stimuli.

sample_responses.response: The research findings refute the main hypothesis, as the hypothesis was that a dog's reaction would differ based on how well they knew the person, and while they did slightly differ, they stayed relatively the same throughout each test.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns 2 points; accurately interprets the 75%/73% finding to argue that the dogs did not discriminate between owner and stranger, refuting the stimulus discrimination hypothesis.

sample_responses.response: The difference between 75% and 73% is not a lot, which refutes that the dogs demonstrate stimulus discrimination, as they did not respond differently to who was crying, the owner or the stranger.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns 2 points; uses the specific 75%/73% data to accurately argue that results refute stimulus discrimination between familiar and unfamiliar persons.
